summary: In this letter to the editor, Frank B. Hu critiques a study for using orthogonal transformation in factor analysis to study insulin resistance syndrome. He argues that forcing metabolic factors to be uncorrelated ignores the underlying biological reality of the syndrome and produces statistical artifacts. Instead, he advocates for using oblique rotation and confirmatory factor analysis to properly test biological theories.

principles.statement: Statistical methods must align with underlying biological theory.
principles.rationale: Using statistically sound methods that force variables to be uncorrelated (like orthogonal rotation) when biological theory dictates they share a common underlying process produces statistical artifacts rather than biological reality.
principles.source_quote: While their approach is statistically sound, the logic is not consistent with the theory of insulin resistance syndrome, which postulates a common underlying biologic process... Thus, the “uncorrelated” factors identified from this study and several previous analyses may merely reflect a statistical artifact rather than a biologic reality.

frameworks.name: Theory-Testing Factor Analysis for Metabolic Clustering
frameworks.when_to_apply: When testing the clustering of metabolic components and a unified mechanism underlying various metabolic abnormalities, rather than just exploring data.
steps: Use an oblique rotation (e.g., promax rotation) to produce correlated factors.
Derive second-order factor(s) by factor analyzing the correlation matrix of the common factors obtained from the first step.
Alternatively, use confirmatory factor analysis to test the ability of the hypothesized factor structure to account for the observed covariance.
Test the equality of factor structure between different groups (e.g., male and female) by comparing the model fit of competing models with and without certain constraints on factor loadings.
frameworks.source_quote: To test the clustering of the components as well as a unified mechanism that underlies various metabolic abnormalities, an alternative rotation method, an oblique rotation... can be used to produce correlated factors. Then second-order factor(s) can be derived... This can also be achieved by confirmatory factor analysis

mental_models.name: Theory-testing vs. Data-driven methods
mental_models.description: A distinction between statistical approaches that merely explore datasets for patterns (data-driven, e.g., explanatory factor analysis) and those that test specific biological hypotheses (theory-testing, e.g., confirmatory factor analysis).
mental_models.example: Using confirmatory factor analysis to test if a hypothesized factor structure accounts for the observed covariance in insulin resistance syndrome, rather than using orthogonal rotation just to achieve clearer interpretability of data.

signature_quotes.text: While their approach is statistically sound, the logic is not consistent with the theory of insulin resistance syndrome, which postulates a common underlying biologic process for the close interrelation among obesity, hyperinsulinemia, glucose intolerance, dyslipidemia, and other metabolic disorders.
signature_quotes.context: Critiquing the use of orthogonal transformation in factor analysis for metabolic factors.

signature_quotes.text: Thus, the “uncorrelated” factors identified from this study and several previous analyses may merely reflect a statistical artifact rather than a biologic reality.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the danger of using statistical methods that contradict biological theory.

signature_quotes.text: ...confirmatory factor analysis, which is a theory-testing method as opposed to a data-driven method like explanatory factor analysis.
signature_quotes.context: Advocating for specific statistical models to test biological hypotheses.

anti_patterns.description: Using orthogonal transformation (like varimax rotation) to force metabolic factors to be uncorrelated in order to achieve clearer interpretability.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It contradicts the biological reality that components of insulin resistance syndrome are inherently intercorrelated due to a common underlying biological process, leading to statistical artifacts.
